---
title: DATASCI 151 - Introduction to Statistical Computing II
subtitle: Lecture 11 - Introduction to SQL
author:
  - name: Danilo Freire
    email: danilo.freire@emory.edu
    affiliations: "Department of Data and Decision Sciences <br> Emory University"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <style>
          #title-slide .title {
            font-size: 42px !important;
          }
          </style>
    code-overflow: wrap
    footer: "[Introduction to SQL](https://raw.githack.com/danilofreire/datasci151-summer/main/lectures/lecture-11/11-introduction-sql.html)"
transition: slide
transition-speed: default
scrollable: true
engine: jupyter
jupyter: datasci
revealjs-plugins:
  - multimodal
  - appearance
editor:
  render-on-save: true
---

# Hey, there! 😊 <br> I hope you're all doing well! {background-color="#2d4563"}

# Today's lecture 📚 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## Today's agenda
### SQL essentials

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 24px;"}
:::{.columns}
::::{.column width="50%"}
- [Introduction to SQL]{.alert}: We will start by learning the fundamentals of SQL, focusing on [SQLite](https://www.sqlite.org/index.html){data-modal-type="iframe"}
- [Setup]{.alert}: You will learn how to:
  - Install and load the SQLite3 Editor in VSCode
  - Create a database
  - Create a table
  - Add and query rows
  - View and modify the table schema
  - Import and export data
:::

::::{.column width="50%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/Sql_data_base_with_logo.png){width="40%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/Sql_data_base_with_logo.png"}

[![](figures/whats-a-relational-database.jpg){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/whats-a-relational-database.jpg"}
:::
:::
:::
:::

# Structured Query Language (SQL) {background-color="#2d4563"}

## SQL: A relational database management system (RDBMS)

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- [SQL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQL){data-modal-type="iframe"} stands for [Structured Query Language]{.alert} and is used to interact with [relational databases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relational_database){data-modal-type="iframe"}
- What is a [relational database]{.alert}?
  - A database that stores data in tables and lets you define relationships between them
  - The most common type of database
  - Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, Oracle, SQL Server
- Here we will focus on [SQLite](https://www.sqlite.org/index.html){data-modal-type="iframe"} 
- Why? It is open-source, powerful, and widely used in industry: phones, browsers, TVs, operating systems, and even cars use it!
- It has several advanced features too, such as [full-text search](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html){data-modal-type="iframe"} and [window functions](https://www.sqlite.org/windowfunctions.html){data-modal-type="iframe"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/sqlite.jpg)](https://www.sqlite.org/index.html)
[![](figures/stack-overflow-survey.png)](https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/technology/#1-databases)
:::
:::
:::
:::

# Let's get started! 🤓 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## What is SQLite?

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- SQLite is a [serverless database]{.alert}: no server needed to run it
- It is [a single file]{.alert} on your machine
- Pros: easy to install, lightweight, fast, great for small projects
- Cons: harder to scale, multiple writers don't play well together (though [LiteFS](https://fly.io/docs/litefs){data-modal-type="iframe"} helps), and time-series data is better served by [TimescaleDB for Postgres](https://www.timescale.com/)
- The [`sqlean` extensions](https://github.com/nalgeon/sqlean) cover many of the gaps
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
- A common myth: SQLite is "not a real database" because it lives in one file
- [This is false]{.alert}: SQLite can handle [Exabyte-scale data](https://sqlite.org/hctree/doc/hctree/doc/hctree/index.html) (1M TB), far more than any classroom project will ever need
- The differences between SQL flavours are mostly small
- [Learn one and you can pick up the others quickly](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-commands.html)
:::
:::
:::

## Installing SQLite
### macOS and Linux

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
- If you use MacOS or Linux, you probably already have SQLite installed
- To check, open a terminal and type `sqlite3` 
  - Then type `.quit` to exit the SQLite prompt!
- If you see the SQLite prompt, you are good to go! 😊
- In case you don't have SQLite installed, you can download it from the [SQLite website](https://www.sqlite.org/download.html) or using [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/) (recommended)
- To install [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/), paste the following command in your terminal

```{verbatim}
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

- Then install SQLite and verify the installation with the following commands

```{verbatim}
brew install sqlite3
sqlite3 --version
```
:::

## Installing SQLite
### Windows

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- Windows users can download the SQLite precompiled binaries from the [SQLite website](https://www.sqlite.org/download.html). Download the files that have the `sqlite3` DLL and the command-line shell (`sqlite-dll...` and `sqlite-tools...`)
- Then follow these steps:
  - Create a folder `C:\>sqlite` and unzip the files in this folder, which will give you `sqlite3.def`, `sqlite3.dll` and `sqlite3.exe` files 
  - Double click the `sqlite3` file to open the software, and you will see the SQLite prompt

```{verbatim}
C:\>sqlite3
SQLite version 3.7.15.2 2013-01-09 11:53:05
Enter ".help" for instructions
Enter SQL statements terminated with a ";"
sqlite>
```
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
- If you want to use SQLite in the command line, you can add the folder to your PATH variable
- Search and click on `Edit the system environment variables`
- Click on `Environment Variables...`
- Set `C:\sqlite` to `Path` in both or either User variables and/or System variables, then you can use SQLite on terminal (command prompt)
- [More about this here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7330565/how-to-install-sqlite-on-windows)
:::
:::
:::

## Installing SQLite3 Editor in VSCode

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="40%"}
- Install the [SQLite3 Editor](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=yy0931.vscode-sqlite3-editor) extension from the VSCode marketplace (or search `SQLite3 Editor` in the Extensions tab and click `Install`)
- Lets you connect to a SQLite database and run SQL inside VSCode, or edit data without writing any SQL at all
- Handles table creation, CSV / JSON export, query running, and `git diff` for database files
- We will use it today to interact with our SQLite database 😊
:::

:::{.column width="60%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/vscode-sqlite.png){width="100%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/vscode-sqlite.png"}
[Download it here](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=yy0931.vscode-sqlite3-editor) or search for it in the extensions tab in VSCode
:::
:::
:::
:::

# Any issues with the installation? 🤔 {background-color="#2d4563"}

# Let's create our first SQL table! 🚀 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## Creating a database with SQLite3 Editor

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 23px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- It is very easy to create a [SQLite database file](https://www.sqlite.org/onefile.html) using the extension
- Simply create a file ending in `.sqlite` or `.db` and open it with the SQLite3 Editor
- Here I will create a file called `example.db` and open it with the extension

```{verbatim}
touch example.db
```

- Then just click on the file (or right-click and select `Open to the Side` -- same thing)
- You will see a new tab with the SQLite3 Editor
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/sqlite-editor.png){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/sqlite-editor.png"}
:::
:::
:::
:::

## Creating a table with SQLite3 Editor

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- Now that you have opened the file with the extension, you will see the following screen (right)
- You will see that there is no table in the database yet
- Most of the time, the data come from other sources, such as APIs, JSON or CSV files
- But in small projects, it is common to create a database to store the data
- So let's create one right now! 😉
- We will create a table with some information about [Formula 1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_One){data-modal-type="iframe"} drivers
- [System commands are in upper case]{.alert} and [user-defined names are in lower case]{.alert}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/create-table.png){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/create-table.png"}
:::
:::
:::
:::

## Creating a table with SQLite3 Editor

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="40%"}
- Now let's create our table
- We won't write any code yet, just add the values to the editor
- We will create four columns in the table: `driver_id`, `driver_name`, `nationality`, and `victories`:
  - `driver_id` is an `integer`, `not null`, and `primary key` (select these options in the editor)
  - `driver_name` is a `text` variable
  - `nationality` is a `text` variable too
  - `victories` is an `integer`
- Then click on `commit` to save the changes
:::

:::{.column width="60%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/add-columns.png){width="100%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/add-columns.png"}
:::

- This is equivalent of the following SQL code (as you can see in the editor):
 
```{sql}
CREATE TABLE drivers (
    driver_id INTEGER NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    driver_name TEXT,
    nationality TEXT,
    victories INTEGER
);
```
:::
:::
:::

## Creating a table with SQLite3 Editor

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px; text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/view-table.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/view-table.png"}
:::

## Database structure and data types in SQLite

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
### Database structure

- The main object in SQL is the [table]{.alert} (rows and columns)
- SQLite is [strongly typed]{.alert}: each column has a specific data type
- Five core data types:
  - [`TEXT`]{.alert}: strings (UTF-8)
  - [`INTEGER`]{.alert}: whole numbers
  - [`NUMERIC`]{.alert}: decimals (floating-point)
  - [`BLOB`]{.alert}: binary data, stored as-is
  - [`NULL`]{.alert}: missing or undefined values
- Plus extras like `REAL`, `BOOLEAN`, `DATE`, `TIME`, `DATETIME`
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
### Primary and foreign keys

- The [`PRIMARY KEY`]{.alert} uniquely identifies each row, cannot be `NULL`, and is usually an integer
- Use [`AUTOINCREMENT`]{.alert} to generate the values for you (`driver_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT`)
- The [`FOREIGN KEY`]{.alert} links two tables: a field in one table that refers to the `PRIMARY KEY` in another
- `ROWID` is a hidden auto-generated column, [but avoid it]{.alert}: it can change after deletions and is not portable across SQL databases
:::
:::
:::

## Example of tables with primary and foreign keys

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- Imagine that we have three tables, `customers`, `products`, and `orders`
- The `customers` table will have a primary key `customer_id`
- The `products` table will have a primary key `product_id`
- And the `orders` table will have a primary key `order_id`
- The `orders` table will also have two foreign keys: `customer_id` and `product_id`
- We will see more about this in the next classes
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
:::{style="text-align: center;"}
Table: customers

| customer_id (PK) | first_name | last_name | email                |
|------------------|------------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1                | John       | Doe       | john.doe@email.com   |
| 2                | Jane       | Smith     | jane.smith@email.com |
| 3                | Bob        | Johnson   | bob.j@email.com      |

Table: products

| product_id (PK) | product_name    | price |
|-----------------|-----------------|-------|
| 101             | Laptop          | 999.99|
| 102             | Smartphone      | 599.99|
| 103             | Wireless Mouse  | 29.99 |

Table: orders

| order_id (PK) | customer_id (FK) | product_id (FK) | quantity | total_price |
|---------------|------------------|-----------------|----------|-------------|
| 1             | 1                | 101             | 1        | 999.99      |
| 2             | 2                | 102             | 2        | 1199.98     |
| 3             | 3                | 103             | 3        | 89.97       |
:::
:::
:::
:::

## Add rows to table using the editor

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; text-align: center; font-size: 22px;"}
[![](figures/add-rows.png){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/add-rows.png"}
:::

## Adding rows to the table using SQL commands

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 21px;"}
- Use the [`INSERT INTO`]{.alert} command to add rows to a table
- It appends new rows (with `VALUES`) to the bottom of the table
- In SQL, different commands need to be separated by a semicolon (`;`)
- Write [system commands in capital letters]{.alert} to distinguish them from column names, variables, and values
- Let's add some information about the drivers
- Click on `Query Editor` and paste the following code:

```{sql}
INSERT INTO drivers VALUES (2, 'Michael Schumacher', 'German', 53);
INSERT INTO drivers VALUES (3, 'Sebastian Vettel', 'German', 91);
INSERT INTO drivers VALUES (4, 'Fernando Alonso', 'Spanish', 32);
```

Or more efficiently:

```{sql}
INSERT INTO drivers (driver_id, driver_name, nationality, victories) VALUES
(2, 'Michael Schumacher', 'German', 53),
(3, 'Sebastian Vettel', 'German', 91),
(4, 'Fernando Alonso', 'Spanish', 32);
```

- Now click on ` ▶️️ Execute` ️to run the code
:::

## Adding rows to the table

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px; text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/added-values.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/added-values.png"}
:::

## Querying the table

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- The editor already shows all the rows in the table, but we can also query the table using the `SELECT` command

```{sql}
SELECT * FROM drivers;
```

- This command selects all columns (`*`) from the `drivers` table
- [`SELECT`]{.alert} is probably the most used command in SQL and is used to extract data from a database
- If you are running SQL in the command line, you need to type `SELECT` to see the results

:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/select-all.png){width="68%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/select-all.png"}
:::
::: 

## Exercise 01 {#sec:exercise01}

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- Click on `Query Editor` and create a table called `students` with the following columns:
  - `student_id` (integer not null primary key)
  - `student_name` (text)
  - `major` (text)
  - `gpa` (numeric)
- Add the following rows to the table:
  - `student_id`: 1, `student_name`: 'John Doe', `major`: 'Computer Science', `gpa`: 3.5
  - `student_id`: 2, `student_name`: 'Jane Doe', `major`: 'Mathematics', `gpa`: 3.8
- Query the table to see the results
- If the table already exists on your database, drop it first with `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;`
- [[Appendix 01]{.button}](#sec:appendix01)
:::

# Table Schemas 📄 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## What is a table schema?

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 23px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- The [table schema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Database_schema){data-modal-type="iframe"} is the [structure of a table]{.alert}: its columns, data types, constraints, and relationships
- Defined when the table is created, but can be modified later (though you usually won't need to)
- View it with the [`PRAGMA table_info()`]{.alert} command
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
`PRAGMA table_info()` returns one row per column with:

- `cid`: column ID
- `name`: column name
- `type`: data type
- `notnull`: 1 if `NULL` is forbidden, 0 otherwise
- `dflt_value`: default value
- `pk`: 1 if part of the primary key, 0 otherwise
:::
:::
:::

:::{style="text-align: center;"}
[![](figures/table-info.png){width="70%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/table-info.png"}
:::

## Creating and modifying table schemas

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 21px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- To create a table, use the [`CREATE TABLE`]{.alert} command, as we saw before
- Use the `IF NOT EXISTS` clause to avoid errors when the table already exists
  - Example: `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS table_name (column1_name data_type, ...);`
- Use [`ALTER TABLE`]{.alert} to modify an existing table:
  - Add a column: `ALTER TABLE table_name ADD column_name data_type;`
  - Drop a column: `ALTER TABLE table_name DROP COLUMN column_name;`
  - Rename a column: `ALTER TABLE table_name RENAME COLUMN old_name TO new_name;`
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
- To delete a table, use the [`DROP TABLE`]{.alert} command
  - Example: `DROP TABLE table_name;`
  - Be careful: this deletes all the data in the table!
- To delete rows, use the [`DELETE FROM`]{.alert} command
  - Example: `DELETE FROM table_name WHERE condition;`
  - Deletes all rows that satisfy the condition
  - Without a condition, it deletes every row in the table
:::
:::
:::

## Some examples with the `students` table

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- Add a column: `ALTER TABLE students ADD COLUMN age INTEGER;`

[![](figures/add-column.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/add-column.png"}

- Drop a column: `ALTER TABLE students DROP COLUMN major;`

[![](figures/drop-column.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/drop-column.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
- Rename a column: `ALTER TABLE students RENAME COLUMN gpa TO grade;`

[![](figures/rename-column.png){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/rename-column.png"}

- Delete a row with `student_id = 2`: `DELETE FROM students WHERE student_id = 2;`

[![](figures/delete-row.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/delete-row.png"}
:::
:::
:::

# Table Operations 📊 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## Extracting columns from table

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
### SELECT extracts certain columns
- The [`SELECT`]{.alert} command extracts certain columns from a table

```{sql}
SELECT driver_id, nationality FROM drivers;
```

[![](figures/select-columns.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/select-columns.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
### Subsetting rows

- The [`WHERE`]{.alert} command evaluates logical conditions to subset rows

```{sql}
SELECT * FROM drivers
WHERE nationality = 'German';
```

[![](figures/where-german.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/where-german.png"}
:::
:::
:::

## AND and OR conditions

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="54%"}
### OR conditions
- Evaluate OR conditions

```{sql}
SELECT * FROM drivers
WHERE (nationality = 'Spanish') OR (nationality = 'German');
```

[![](figures/or-conditions.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/or-conditions.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="46%"}
### AND conditions
- Evaluate AND conditions

```{sql}
SELECT * FROM drivers
WHERE (nationality = 'German') AND (driver_id = 3);
```

[![](figures/and-conditions.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/and-conditions.png"}
:::
:::
:::

## Data description

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
### Aggregate

- To compute aggregate statistics, use a statistic function such as `SUM()` or `AVG()`
- Use [`AS`]{.alert} to give the aggregate statistic a name (an *alias*)
  
```{sql}
SELECT SUM(victories) AS sum_victories,
       COUNT(*) AS num_rows,
       AVG(victories) AS mean_victories,
       MIN(victories) AS min_victories,
       MAX(victories) AS max_victories
FROM drivers;
```

[![](figures/aggregate.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/aggregate.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
### Sort

- Use [`ORDER BY`]{.alert} to sort the data
- Use `ASC` for ascending order and `DESC` for descending

```{sql}
SELECT driver_name, victories FROM drivers
ORDER BY victories ASC;
```

[![](figures/order-asc.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/order-asc.png"}
:::
:::
:::

## Exercise 02 {#sec:exercise02}

:::{style="margin-top: 30px; font-size: 26px;"}
- List drivers who are either 'German' or 'British' and have victories greater than 50
- List all drivers with victories greater than the average victories 
  - Hint: you will have to run another `SELECT` command to get the average victories (a subquery)
  - Aggregate functions are not allowed in the `WHERE` clause. [See the reason why here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42470849/why-are-aggregate-functions-not-allowed-in-where-clause). [More about subqueries here.](https://mode.com/sql-tutorial/sql-sub-queries)
  - We will see another way to do this soon (with `HAVING`)
- [[Appendix 02]{.button}](#sec:appendix02)
:::

## Groupby + Aggregate

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
### Groupby

- Use [`GROUP BY`]{.alert} to group data by a column

```{sql}
SELECT nationality,
       SUM(victories) AS sum_victories,
       AVG(victories) AS mean_victories,
       MIN(victories) AS min_victories,
       MAX(victories) AS max_victories
FROM drivers
GROUP BY nationality;
```

[![](figures/groupby.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/groupby.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
### Round

- Use [`ROUND()`]{.alert} to round the values
- The first argument is the value to round, the second is the number of decimal places
- The results match the previous slide, except that `mean_victories` is now rounded to one decimal place

```{sql}
SELECT nationality,
       SUM(victories) AS sum_victories,
       ROUND(AVG(victories), 1) AS mean_victories,
       MIN(victories) AS min_victories,
       MAX(victories) AS max_victories
FROM drivers
GROUP BY nationality;
```

[![](figures/round.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/round.png"}
:::
:::
:::

## Filtering after aggregating with HAVING

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- [`HAVING`]{.alert} filters rows [after aggregating]{.alert}
- [`WHERE`]{.alert} filters rows [before aggregating]{.alert}
- Position in the query: `HAVING` goes after `GROUP BY` and before `ORDER BY`

```{sql}
SELECT nationality,
       SUM(victories) AS sum_victories,
       ROUND(AVG(victories), 1) AS mean_victories,
       MIN(victories) AS min_victories,
       MAX(victories) AS max_victories
FROM drivers
GROUP BY nationality
HAVING SUM(victories) > 50;
```

[![](figures/having.png){width="90%"}](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/having.png"}
:::

## Python vs. SQL

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 21px;"}
- Common SQL commands and their pandas equivalents:

| SQL command   | Pandas equivalent                                                              |
|---------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `SELECT`      | `df[['column1', 'column2']]`                                                   |
| `INSERT INTO` | `pd.concat([df, pd.DataFrame([new_row])], ignore_index=True)`                  |
| `WHERE`       | `df[df['column'] == value]`                                                    |
| `GROUP BY`    | `df.groupby('column')`                                                         |
| `HAVING`      | `df.groupby('column').filter(lambda x: condition)`                             |
| Aggregations  | `df.groupby('column').agg({'col1': 'sum', 'col2': 'mean'})`                    |

<br>

:::{style="font-size: 20px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="40%"}
```{sql}
-- SQL
SELECT nationality,
       SUM(victories) AS sum_victories
FROM drivers
GROUP BY nationality
HAVING SUM(victories) > 10;
```
:::

:::{.column width="60%"}
```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
# Python
import pandas as pd

# Group by 'nationality' and calculate 'sum_victories' using agg()
result = (
    drivers.groupby('nationality')
    .agg(sum_victories=('victories', 'sum'))
    .reset_index()
)

# Apply 'HAVING' condition
result = result[result['sum_victories'] > 10]
```
:::
:::
:::
:::

## Importing and exporting data

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
- The SQLite3 Editor imports and exports data in CSV, JSON, and XLSX formats. Click `Other Tools` → `Export / Import`
- For XLSX, you can export one table or all of them at once
- Importing is just as easy: pick the file and choose which table to load into
- From the command line:
  - Export to CSV: `sqlite3 example.db .mode csv .output example.csv "SELECT * FROM drivers;"` ([guide](https://www.sqlitetutorial.net/sqlite-export-csv/){data-modal-type="iframe"})
  - Import from CSV: `sqlite3 example.db .mode csv .import example.csv drivers` ([guide](https://www.sqlitetutorial.net/sqlite-import-csv/){data-modal-type="iframe"})
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
[![](figures/export-import.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/export-import.png"}
:::
:::
:::

## Do you want to learn more? 🤓 

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- Here is a list of resources to help you learn more about SQL:
  - [Overview of the SQLite3 Extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=yy0931.vscode-sqlite3-editor): Worth reading to understand all the features of the extension
  - [SQLean](https://github.com/nalgeon/sqlean): SQLite extensions. MacOS users should install the [sqlean.py](https://antonz.org/sqlean-py/) Python package, as sqlite3 is compiled without extension support
  - [SQLBolt](https://sqlbolt.com/): Interactive SQL tutorials
  - [W3Schools](https://www.w3schools.com/sql/): SQL tutorials
  - [Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/intro-to-sql): SQL course
  - [Mode Analytics](https://mode.com/sql-tutorial/): SQL tutorial
  - [SQLZoo](https://sqlzoo.net/): Interactive SQL tutorials
  - [SQL for Data Science](https://www.datacamp.com/courses/sql-for-data-science): DataCamp course
  - [SQL for Data Science](https://www.coursera.org/learn/sql-for-data-science): Coursera course

- Or just come to our next class! 😊
:::

# And that's it for today! 🚀 {background-color="#2d4563"}

# See you next time! 😊 {background-color="#2d4563"}

## Appendix 01 {#sec:appendix01}

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- Here is the code to create the `students` table and add the rows
- You can paste this code into the SQL editor and execute the commands

```{sql}
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS students;

CREATE TABLE students(
    student_id INTEGER NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    student_name TEXT,
    major TEXT,
    gpa NUMERIC
);

INSERT INTO students VALUES 
(1, 'John Doe', 'Computer Science', 3.5),
(2, 'Jane Doe', 'Mathematics', 3.8);

SELECT * FROM students;
```

- [[Back to exercise]{.button}](#sec:exercise01)
:::

## Appendix 02 {#sec:appendix02}

:::{style="margin-top: 50px; font-size: 22px;"}
- Here is the code to list drivers who are either 'German' or 'British' and have victories greater than 50

```{sql}
SELECT driver_name, nationality, victories
FROM drivers
WHERE (nationality = 'German' OR nationality = 'British') AND victories > 50;

SELECT driver_name, victories
FROM drivers
WHERE victories > (SELECT AVG(victories) FROM drivers);
```

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width="50%"}
[![](figures/app02-01.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/app02-01.png"}
:::

:::{.column width="50%"}
[![](figures/app02-02.png)](#){data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="figures/app02-02.png"}
:::
:::

- [[Back to exercise]{.button}](#sec:exercise02)
:::
